Bronchoalveolar Lavage in Other Interstitial
Lung Diseases

Ulrich Costabel, M.D.,¹ Josune Guzman, M.D.,² Francesco Bonella, M.D.,¹
and Shinichiro Oshimo, M.D.¹





   ABSTRACT

                                      This article reviews the changes in bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) cytology and
                              cell differentials in some of the rarer interstitial lung diseases. In a few of these diseases
                              BAL has a diagnostic value and can replace lung biopsy. In pulmonary Langerhans cell
                              histiocytosis the characteristic diagnostic ﬁnding is an increase in CD1 þ Langerhans
                              cells greater than 4% of total cells. The sensitivity of this cutoff value is low because only
                              50% of patients show this elevation. In pulmonary alveolar proteinosis, the sensitivity
                              of a diagnostic BAL is almost 100%, and the characteristic ﬁnding of milky and turbid
                              ﬂuid on gross examination and the characteristic ﬁndings with acellular globules that
                              stain pink with PAS (periodic‐acid‐Schiff), along with abnormal foamy macrophages
                              and a characteristic dirty background obviates the need for lung biopsy. In diffuse
                              alveolar hemorrhage, BAL is the method of choice to diagnose the alveolar bleeding by
                              showing free red blood cells and hemosiderin‐laden, iron‐positive macrophages. The
                              underlying disorder has to be identiﬁed by history and clinical and laboratory tests. In
                              eosinophilic lung disease, the diagnosis can be made if the BAL cell differentials show
                              25% or more eosinophils. In collagen vascular disease–associated lung ﬁbrosis, the
                              precise role of BAL in assessment and monitoring disease remains unclear. In drug‐
                              induced interstitial lung disease BAL may support a certain clinical/pathological pattern
                              of lung involvement and is helpful for exclusion of other diseases, such as malignancies
                              with pulmonary metastasis, heart disease with pulmonary congestion, or infections. The
                              same is true for radiation‐induced lung injury.

                              KEYWORDS: Bronchoalveolar lavage, Langerhans cell histiocytosis, pulmonary
                              alveolar proteinosis, diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, eosinophile lung disease,
                              drug-induced lung disease

Bronchoalveolar Lavage in Other Interstitial Lung Diseases

# Bronchoalveolar Lavage in Other Interstitial Lung Diseases

Ulrich Costabel, M.D.,¹ Josune Guzman, M.D.,² Francesco Bonella, M.D.,¹ and Shinichiro Oshimo, M.D.¹

# ABSTRACT

This article reviews the changes in bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) cytology and cell differentials in some of the rarer interstitial lung diseases. In a few of these diseases BAL has a diagnostic value and can replace lung biopsy. In pulmonary Langerhans cell histiocytosis the characteristic diagnostic finding is an increase in CD1 + Langerhans cells greater than 4% of total cells. The sensitivity of this cutoff value is low because only 50% of patients show this elevation. In pulmonary alveolar proteinosis, the sensitivity of a diagnostic BAL is almost 100%, and the characteristic finding of milky and turbid fluid on gross examination and the characteristic findings with acellular globules that stain pink with PAS (periodic‐acid‐Schiff), along with abnormal foamy macrophages and a characteristic dirty background obviates the need for lung biopsy. In diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, BAL is the method of choice to diagnose the alveolar bleeding by showing free red blood cells and hemosiderin‐laden, iron‐positive macrophages. The underlying disorder has to be identified by history and clinical and laboratory tests. In eosinophilic lung disease, the diagnosis can be made if the BAL cell differentials show 25% or more eosinophils. In collagen vascular disease–associated lung fibrosis, the precise role of BAL in assessment and monitoring disease remains unclear. In drug‐induced interstitial lung disease BAL may support a certain clinical/pathological pattern of lung involvement and is helpful for exclusion of other diseases, such as malignancies with pulmonary metastasis, heart disease with pulmonary congestion, or infections. The same is true for radiation‐induced lung injury.

# KEYWORDS

Bronchoalveolar lavage, Langerhans cell histiocytosis, pulmonary alveolar proteinosis, diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, eosinophile lung disease, drug-induced lung disease

This article reviews the changes in bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) cytology and cell differentials in some of the rarer interstitial lung diseases (ILDs). In a few of these diseases BAL has a diagnostic value and can replace lung biopsy. Most of these disorders are included in the group of alveolar filling syndromes. Obviously, the abnormal material that accumulates in the alveolar spaces in these syndromes can easily be washed out by BAL and is so characteristic that a specific BAL diagnosis is possible.

# PULMONARY LANGERHANS CELL HISTIOCYTOSIS

Pulmonary Langerhans cell histiocytosis is a rare, smoking‐related, chronic granulomatous disorder.

pages.md:
BAL IN OTHER INTERSTITIAL LUNG DISEASES/COSTABEL ET AL

characterized by focal bronchiolocentric infiltrates of Langerhans cells mixed with eosinophils and cyst formation. Previous designations were histiocytosis X and eosinophilic granuloma.

The central pathogenetic feature of pulmonary Langerhans cell histiocytosis is thought to be an abnormal immune response to cigarette smoke, initiated and maintained by Langerhans cells. Langerhans cells are present in small numbers in many normal tissues, especially the skin. In the normal lung, they are only seen in the airway epithelium. Langerhans cells are similar to dendritic cells but, in contrast to dendritic cells, are characterized by typical cytoplasmic organelles (X-bodies or Birbeck granules) visible with electron microscopy. Immunostaining shows CD1 antigen on their cell surface and S-100 protein in the cytoplasm. They also express a Langerhans cell–specific lectin called Langerin. Langerhans cells act as antigen-presenting cells and stimulate lymphocyte proliferation.

# PULMONARY ALVEOLAR PROTEINOSIS

Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis (PAP) is a rare disorder characterized by abundant accumulation of surfactant-derived phospholipids and protein components within the alveoli. A recent major advance is the discovery of neutralizing anti–granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF) antibodies in the serum and BAL fluid of patients with acquired pulmonary alveolar proteinosis, suggesting that the disease has an autoimmune pathogenesis. The neutralizing autoantibody blocks the activity of endogenous GM-CSF, thereby causing an impairment of the surfactant-clearing function of alveolar macrophages. Normally, phagocytosis and degradation by alveolar macrophages of surfactant aggregates is critically dependent on GM-CSF that activates the alveolar macrophages and increases their rate of surfactant clearance. This function is blocked in PAP.

BAL as a research tool has provided insights into the composition of the abnormal material and into the abnormal functions of alveolar macrophages in PAP. Biochemical analysis of the material demonstrated that total phospholipids are increased, with a relative decrease in phosphatidylcholine and phosphatidylglycerol, and a relative increase in sphingomyelin and phosphatidylinositol. Also, increased concentrations of the surfactant proteins A, B, and D have been found in BAL fluid and serum. These studies confirmed that the accumulated alveolar material is surfactant derived. Various tumor markers, such as carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and CA19–9, have been reported to be elevated in BAL fluid and serum of patients with PAP. Alveolar macrophages recovered from BAL fluid show several functional defects, such as reduced mobility, impaired.

SEMINARS IN RESPIRATORY AND CRITICAL CARE MEDICINE/VOLUME 28, NUMBER 5 2007

# Diagnosis of Pulmonary Alveolar Proteinosis

| Useful for Diagnosis  | Suggests an Alternative Diagnosis                                                                                                                       |
| --------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Clinical features     | None                                                                                                                                                    |
| Radiological findings | Chest radiograph: butterfly pattern of acinar shadowing HRCT: geographical distribution of a "crazy paving" pattern                                     |
| BAL findings          | Gross aspect: milky fluid; BAL cytology: PAS positive Absence of the pathognomonic BAL findings, few and foamy macrophages, large amount of cell debris |
| Laboratory tests      | LDH increased (nonspecific), CEA elevated, GM-CSF autoantibody positive (not yet routine test)                                                          |

BAL, bronchoalveolar lavage; CEA, carcinoembryonic antigen; GM-CSF, granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor; HRCT, high-resolution computed tomography; LDH, lactate dehydrogenase; PAS, periodic acid-Schiff.

Adherence and chemotaxis, reduced phagocytosis, and decreased ability to kill ingested microorganisms. The functional impairment of macrophages may contribute to the increased risk of pulmonary infections associated with PAP. BAL has a high diagnostic value in PAP. The diagnosis is usually established by BAL, obviating the need for transbronchial or open biopsy in many instances. On gross examination, the BAL fluid is milky and turbid. This is so characteristic that the diagnosis can be suspected in the bronchoscopy room. Light microscopy reveals three striking features:

- Characteristic acellular globules that are basophilic on May-Grünwald-Giemsa staining and pink with PAS staining, but do not stain with alcian blue, differentiating them from mucins.
- Abnormal "foamy" macrophages engorged with diastase-resistant PAS-positive intracellular inclusions.
- A characteristic dirty background due to large amounts of amorphous debris showing weak PAS staining, representing myelin-like multilamellated structures and lamellar bodies.

Electron microscopy for confirmation is not necessary in the routine clinical setting, but if done shows abundant lamellar bodies and cellular debris. Cell differentials may show a nonspecific increase in lymphocytes with the tendency toward an elevated CD4:CD8 ratio.

# DIFFUSE ALVEOLAR HEMORRHAGE

Diffuse alveolar hemorrhage (DAH) refers to a clinical syndrome with widespread bleeding into distal airspaces resulting from microvascular injury, frequently associated with an immunologic or autoimmune disorder and causing red blood cell accumulation and iron deposition in the deep lung. DAH is defined by the clinical triad of hemoptysis, anemia, and progressive hypoxemia. However, hemoptysis may be absent in up to one third of patients, and then the diagnosis is frequently delayed.

Several conditions are associated with DAH, and the underlying disease determines the prognosis and the treatment modalities. Because extensive DAH can be life threatening, early recognition is important and may improve the outcome. If DAH is suspected, the diagnosis must first be confirmed, and then an underlying disorder should be identified.

Diagnosis of DAH by BAL is possible in every patient, even if the bleeding is occult.

# Table 4 Golde Score for the Quantification of Alveolar Hemorrhage

| Hemosiderin | Blue Staining of Macrophage Cytoplasm                 |
| ----------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| 0           | No blue color                                         |
| 1           | Faint staining                                        |
| 2           | Dense in minor portion or medium throughout cytoplasm |

| 4           | Dark blue throughout cytoplasm                        |

a200 to 300 macrophages are counted, and each cell is graded on the Golde scale. The mean score for 100 cells is calculated, with 400 being the maximum (if all cells are graded with 4).

The recovered BAL fluid becomes progressively more bloody with each sequential sample. This is characteristic of alveolar hemorrhage and is not seen when the blood is aspirated from the central airways (then the first fraction is the most bloody one). In severe hemorrhage, more than 90% of the macrophages are hemosiderin laden (i.e., siderophages) as characterized by a positive iron stain. The severity of the bleeding can be quantified using the Golde score (Table 4). Golde and colleagues proposed a numerical scale based on the blue intensity of macrophages on an iron stain graded from 0 to 4. The maximum score is 400 and a normal score is below 20. Scores between 20 and 100 indicate mild, between 100 and 300 moderate, and greater than 300 severe bleeding. The efficacy of this Golde score was validated by Kahn et al in patients with new pulmonary infiltrates, and the Golde score correlated closely with the histologic severity of DAH. The Golde score is very time consuming, however, and difficult for routine use. Therefore, in routine use, the percentage of siderophages is counted, and it has been shown that a percentage of siderophages equal to or greater than 20% is sufficient for diagnosis of DAH. In the clinical setting, chronic left heart failure with pulmonary congestion is one of the most frequent underlying conditions for the finding of DAH in BAL fluid examination, based on our own experience. Importantly, hemosiderin-laden macrophages do not appear earlier than 48 hours after bleeding. Thus very early bleeding only shows numerous red blood cells. Animal experiments and human studies have shown that, after a single episode of acute alveolar hemorrhage, the following chronological changes can be observed:

1. Within the first hours, the only evidence is a high number of red blood cells; the macrophages still appear normal.
2. Within 48 hours, the macrophages show roundish, yellow-brown fragments of phagocytosed red blood cells.

3. Not earlier than 48 to 70 hours, hemosiderin, a hemoglobulin degradation product, becomes apparent in the macrophages and can be observed for several months after the bleeding has ceased. 64,65 Another important point is to differentiate exogenous iron load of the lungs from endogenous bleeding. Exogenous iron load may be caused by inhalation of iron dust particles in certain occupations, such as in metal grinding and welding. Exogenous siderosis can be differentiated by showing irregularly shaped dust particles engulfed by macrophages and not the roundish fragments of erythrocytes within the cytoplasm of the macrophages. 67–70

After having established the diagnosis of DAH, clinical investigations and laboratory tests, including ANA, ANCA, and anti-GBM antibodies, may allow the diagnosis of the underlying condition. If an underlying systemic disease can be established, surgical lung biopsy is not necessary. However, this procedure should be strongly considered in younger patients who have isolated pulmonary hemorrhage without clinical or serological evidence of a systemic disease.

# COLLAGEN VASCULAR DISEASE

# EOSINOPHILIC LUNG DISEASE

Eosinophilic lung diseases are a heterogeneous group of disorders characterized by infiltration of the lung tissue with eosinophils. 71 They may be associated with or without peripheral blood eosinophilia. Some of these disorders are systemic diseases, others are localized to the lungs. There are many possible causes, such as infections, drug-induced reactions, and allergic diseases such as allergic bronchopulmonary mycoses. Some of them are of undetermined origin and are labeled idiopathic eosinophilic lung disorders, including idiopathic chronic eosinophilic pneumonia, idiopathic acute eosinophilic pneumonia, Churg-Strauss syndrome and hypereosinophilic syndrome. Other diseases with minor pulmonary eosinophilia include asthma, eosinophilic bronchitis, organizing pneumonia, idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis and other idiopathic interstitial pneumonias, PLCH, lymphomas, and other malignancies. 72

Based on the limited numbers of BAL studies in CVD diseases, many of them combining different rheumatological diseases, the precise role of BAL in the assessment and monitoring of pulmonary manifestations of CVD remains unclear. Initial reports suggested that patients with an abnormal BAL cell differential (increase in lymphocytes and neutrophils) without clinical or radiological disease (the concept of “subclinical alveolitis”) would later develop progressive pulmonary fibrosis. 78–83

The clinical diagnosis of eosinophilic pneumonia is usually made by the combination of pulmonary infiltrates on imaging and increased eosinophil counts in blood and/or BAL fluid. BAL is now a widely accepted and minimally invasive technique to establish the diagnosis of eosinophilic pneumonia. 71–73 The diagnostic contribution is particularly helpful if the level of blood eosinophils is normal. In clinical practice, the diagnosis of eosinophilic pneumonia can be made if the BAL cell differential shows 25% or more eosinophils. 71 The mean value SD in our laboratory in patients with eosinophilic infiltrates is 46 22%, and the eosinophil count is usually higher than the neutrophil count. In addition, a normal BAL finding generally excludes an eosinophilic infiltrate.

pages.md:
# BAL IN OTHER INTERSTITIAL LUNG DISEASES/COSTABEL ET AL

# Systemic Sclerosis

A large study of BAL cell findings in systemic sclerosis reported data from 73 patients, and increases in neutrophils and/or eosinophils and/or lymphocytes were seen. More eosinophils were observed in patients with an NSIP pattern in contrast to those with a UIP/end-stage lung pattern of disease. Neutrophils and lymphocytes did not differ between these two subgroups. Within the NSIP pattern, those with the cellular type showed more lymphocytes than those with the fibrotic type. The same group of authors had previously shown that increased BAL neutrophils are associated with extensive changes on HRCT, especially with reticular abnormalities, whereas eosinophils were increased in less extensive disease as well as in extensive disease on HRCT. This indicates that the differential cell counts in systemic sclerosis are of questionable prognostic value because increases in BAL granulocytes, having been linked to a poor outcome in previous studies, are associated with more extensive fibrosis on HRCT and may be indicative of severe disease, which is probably more likely to progress.

# Sjögren Syndrome

An early study found the major abnormality in lung involvement of Sjögren syndrome is an increase in lymphocytes. Some patients show an additional increase in neutrophils. A longitudinal study of pulmonary involvement in Sjögren syndrome in a small cohort of 18 individuals who were lavaged a second time after a 2-year interval showed that lymphocytosis is prominent and usually associated with good outcome, whereas neutrophils, when present, are associated with persistent interstitial lung disease.

# Polymyositis/Dermatomyositis

The few published studies reported increases in neutrophils or lymphocytes or both in BAL. The largest, recently published study identified diffuse lung disease in 20 of the 63 consecutive patients. Ten of the 20 patients had progressive lung disease and showed BAL neutrophilia and extensive ground-glass opacities on HRCT. The remaining 10 patients without progressive lung disease did not show an abnormal BAL cell differential. This may indicate that increased neutrophils are associated with progression of ILD in patients with dermatomyositis/polymyositis.

# Other Connective Tissue Diseases

Due to the paucity of studies, it is not possible to draw conclusions on the use of BAL in systemic lupus erythematosus, undifferentiated connective tissue diseases, or ankylosing spondylitis.

# DRUG-INDUCED INTERSTITIAL LUNG DISEASE

A large number of drugs are capable of inducing an interstitial lung reaction, either toxic or immune mediated. In addition to toxic changes of the type II pneumocytes, any type of alveolitis (lymphocytic, neutrophilic, eosinophilic, or mixed) or diffuse alveolar hemorrhage may be evident in BAL. The most frequent change is a lymphocytic alveolitis with a predominance of CD8+ cells, very similar to extrinsic allergic alveolitis. In methotrexate-induced pneumonitis, the CD4+ cells may be preferentially increased; this has also been demonstrated for ampicillin, nitrofurantoin, and sirolimus. Amiodarone induces characteristic alterations in the cytoplasm of the alveolar macrophage population with foamy vacuoles, corresponding to a form of.

# RADIATION-INDUCED LUNG INJURY

Radiation‐induced lung damage is a limiting factor for radiotherapy not only of primary lung tumors but also of those arising in the breast, mediastinum, and chest wall, as well as for total body irradiation in hematological malignancies. Beside the classical well‐recognized syndromes related to thoracic irradiation that occur in the radiation field (i.e., radiation pneumonitis and radiation fibrosis), three other syndromes have been recognized, occurring outside the field of radiation: BOOP, bilateral lymphocytic alveolitis, and eosinophilic pneumonia.118–122

In regard to pathogenesis, two separate and distinct mechanisms are likely involved123: First, the classical radiation pneumonitis that leads to pulmonary fibrosis. Here the primary injury is to pulmonary endothelial cells and type II pneumocytes by the generation of free radicals that damage cell membranes and DNA. This is a toxic mechanism. Second, the radiation‐primed inflammation outside the radiation field, resulting in bilateral lymphocytic alveolitis or BOOP. Here the process is immunologically mediated, with an increase in activated T‐lymphocytes in BAL fluid suggestive of hypersensitivity pneumonitis.

Pulmonary infiltrates with eosinophilia or eosinophilic pneumonia are also associated with many drugs, and BAL shows significant eosinophilia; the absence of BAL eosinophilia is rather exclusive of pulmonary eosinophilic infiltrates.116

Organizing pneumonia also termed bronchiolitis obliterans organizing pneumonia (BOOP) is another drug‐induced pattern, and BAL shows the usual finding of BOOP, a mixed pattern with increases in lymphocytes, neutrophils, eosinophils, and mast cells; foamy alveolar macrophages and a small number of plasma cells can also be observed, and the CD4:CD8 ratio is low.74

A cytotoxic reaction, associated with a cytostatic chemotherapy, may show histological changes similar to DAD, and BAL may show clusters of atypical type II pneumocytes and a marked increase in neutrophils.117

DAH is induced by a few drugs, and BAL is able to demonstrate the hemosiderin‐laden alveolar macrophages and/or the presence of free red blood cells.

A major role of BAL in patients suspected of having a drug‐induced lung disease is to exclude other diseases. Many of these patients have underlying disorders that can induce pulmonary infiltrates, such as malignancies with pulmonary metastases or heart disease with pulmonary congestion. Patients treated with immunosuppressives or cytostatic chemotherapy are at risk of infections, and microbiological examination of the BAL fluid can establish bacterial, fungal, or viral infections.
